definition: Wage-price spiral risk is very low (<10-15% probability) because the conjunctive institutional prerequisites — high union density, broad COLA coverage, sustained sub-3.8% unemployment, wage growth >5%, and expectations de-anchoring — are absent, with only 2 true spirals occurring in the entire US post-war period.
evidence: Union density ~10% vs. 25-30% in the 1970s; COLA coverage <10% vs. >60%
True wage-price spirals occurred only twice post-war (1966-69, 1973-75)
2021-23 natural experiment: 2/3 conditions temporarily met without triggering spiral
open_questions: Through which alternative channels (fiscal, commodity, shelter, expectations drift) can inflation persist without the spiral mechanism?
